items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.

items.correct: Volume of air inhaled or exhaled in one normal breath at rest.
items.misconception: The maximum air your lungs can hold.
items.random_wrong: Air left in the lungs after maximum exhalation.
items.why: Students confuse tidal volume (resting breath, ~0.5L) with vital capacity (max breath, ~4-5L). Different lung volumes serve different purposes - tidal is the normal small ebb and flow.

items.q: What does the 'S' in SPORT stand for?
items.correct: Specificity - training should match the demands of the sport.
items.why: Students guess fitness components. SPORT = Specificity, Progression, Overload, Reversibility, Tedium (or Time). Specificity means training matches what the athlete needs (sprinter doesn't train like a marathoner).

items.q: How many cervical vertebrae are there?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 7. “12” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Bone adaptation to load. “Lung volumes” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Total vertebrae in the human spine?
items.why: The real figure is 33 vertebrae. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
